item.title: Weeknotes 202623
item.description:
• At work, I hit the ground running on Monday, having prepared myself for it during the break, and had a mountain to catch up on as soon as I arrived. In our team, we are working frantically towards an important mid-June delivery and needs must.
  • I was faced with doing a ~350 TB SQL query (~£1,600) to get to one of our objectives. It's not going to happen, so I will have to come up with a creative solution next week, which honestly is the sort of stuff that makes me get out of bed in the morning, go through the brutal battle of getting the girls ready to go out in a timely fashion, and walk across the landing to the home office, anyway.
  • H. perfected her impersonation of a line we like by a minor character of a well-known cartoon show. It's brilliant—one of the funniest things in the world right now.
  • Weeknotes are supposed to be a quick, raw stream of consciousness, but even so, I need to start doing some basic proofreading before I publish.

item.title: Weeknotes 202622
item.description:
• It seems we've avoided some toasty days in the UK. The temperatures haven't been that different in Lisbon, but it's far more tolerable here. How come Estoril is 23 km from Lisbon but it's always 4 C colder? Is it the ocean breeze?
  • I could have spent my late evenings more unwisely than by reading the entire latest encyclical letter by Pope Leo XIV (via, e.g., Simon Willison). That is an understatement, of course; I thought the reflection about what sort of post-AI world we'd rather have and his view that when we apply AI (or any other technology), we should optimise for the dignity of the human person were very pertinent.
  • Sorry, most people have AI coming out of their ears already but Kyla Scanlon's latest, long and well researched newsletter about AI and jobs was also an interesting read.
  • I have migrated to Antigravity CLI but noticed that it makes changes without showing the diff and seeking confirmation. It's true that otherwise it would be a clunky workflow and not very high-throughput, but are we being encouraged to just vibe code it?
  • The return flight from Lisbon to Stansted was perhaps not as smooth as the outbound flight (take off delays but that's par for the course in Lisbon) but it was completely drama free, which makes this route a valid option for us to visit Lisbon, in addition to Easyjet from Luton.

item.title: Weeknotes 202621
item.description:
• This was an important and busy week. The momentum was interrupted by this week's half-term break, but let's hope I can keep it going when I get back.
  • As I write this, we are in Estoril, the return flight is yet to happen and \(N=1\), but flying to Lisbon with Ryanair from Stansted was quite straightforward—with perhaps the exception of the shuttle transfer between the Long Stay Car Park and the terminal. In comparison to Luton Airport's, it had far longer queues, but it's a bigger airport and it's half-term.
  • Yet again, I felt short changed by the car hire at the Lisbon Airport.
  • We've met with friends for an early dinner on Sunday and it was lovely to catch up.

item.title: Weeknotes 202620
item.description:
• The girls are asleep. I came online and thought about saying hi.
  • Did I really write that I was going to have some interesting weeks ahead??
  • Besides the cosmological, has anything more mundane happened? Let me see.
  • The joys of assembling flat-packed furniture, once again. We configured a Tylko bookshelf on their website (#MyTylko in the socials) and had it delivered all the way from Poland. We're happy with the purchase; the living room looks a bit more civilised now.
  • H. had her first tooth fall out. It wasn't gory at all.
  • A's tricycle was delivered in the meantime. It was initially ignored, as her sister's scooter was still the centre of her life, but with some insistence, it seems to be her first choice now—much to my relief.
  • We're taking a break during the half-term and spending the week in Portugal. A different airport and airline this time, and we've rented an Airbnb for the first time. Let's see how it goes.

item.title: Weeknotes 202615
item.description:
• It was the second week of Easter holidays for H. and we decided to take the week off and spend it in the Center Parcs in the Elveden Forest, Suffolk.
  • It is very much part of the family holidays industrial-complex and it is very formatted but we liked the format and would definitely go back again. Two and five year olds are only able to enjoy about a tenth of what is on offer, so there is still lots of replayability potential.
  • H. definitely had a blast: "Best holidays ever!". Good, that's what we want to hear.
  • I was hoping to do loads of stuff and move the site from beta to something more stable and complete in the late evenings whilst staying there but the competition from reading The Screwtape Letters and watching the end of the season 3 of The Chosen (mostly this), meant things are pretty much in the same state. No worries, we'll get there soon.
  • Placed an order for a tricycle for our two year old A., who is a bit of a Schumacher, and another helmet for H., one size up.
  • Now, it's time to go back to the routine of work, school and nursery. My office day count is at zero in April so it's going to be an interesting rest of the month.

item.title: Weeknotes 202614
item.description:
• The first week of H's Easter holidays. H. went to holiday club on work days, which worked reasonably well. The only downside was the pickup at 1630, meaning that work was somewhat disrupted from that time onwards. I tried to compress the days as much as possible and I think that worked well.
  • I went just once to the office this week and completed the monthly minimum for March. No contribution towards the April count means the second half of the month is going to be a crunch.
  • Next week, we'll take the week off and we're going for the first time to Center Parcs. A very welcome break because energy was running low and we need to manage it until the longer summer break, which will perhaps happen only in August.

item.title: Weeknotes 202613
item.description:
• The site is still in beta. I haven't had the time to address what is still pending because...
  • Last week was different because A. got hands, foot and mouth for the third time and had to stay at home recovering for two days, with my wife and I alternating with the nursing duties. Because she had a blocked nose, A. did not sleep much due to the troubled breathing, so we ended the week exhausted. Things are coming back to normal.
  • I finally seem to have solved a problem with the Pixelbook that has been nagging me since I've started using Linux on it. The variant with an Intel Core i5 CPU (the one I have) doesn't have a modern SSD; it has an eMMC, which is basically an SD card soldered to the motherboard that can only do one operation at a time. This means the system is very limited in terms of I/O. When running I/O intensive tasks like e.g. upgrading the system, that completely saturates the system and even low level events like refreshing what is displayed on the screen or moving the mouse cursor around has to wait. This means that sometimes, especially when these processes would run unattended in the background, the laptop would freeze for minutes on end, rendering it completely unusable. The solution is mainly using a kernel scheduler that deprioritises this sort of tasks and reserves resource for anything essential. This way, even if these I/O heavy tasks may take longer to complete, you can ensure that your system will always be responsive. I may add something about this soon, in a page somewhere.
  • We've got to the end of the first quarter of the year, surely surprising many skeptics. It's time to look again at the yearly objectives and make sure things don't start to slide.

item.title: Weeknotes 202511
item.description:
• It was today the first Formula 1 Grand Prix of the season in Melbourne. I’m watching it as I write this week’s notes. It is being quite entertaining. The McLaren seems to be the class of the field, confirming the suspicions of the pre-season testing. It seems this year’s car is very good at not putting too much strain in the tires, slowing the rate of decay in performance and allowing the drivers to push for longer.
  • I was confident at the end of last year that very soon I’d have more time and energy because nights with the 1 year old were going to improve but it’s already mid-March and it seems the year is flying by and I haven’t crossed anything from the 2025 objectives yet.
  • I have been listening to the remix of Severance soundtrack, Music To Refine To, while working and it’s pretty good. I’ve never watched the show but everything I’ve seen about it looks great, right up my alley. On the other hand, I get the sense it would be a depressing watch as the premise hits too close to home.
  • There’ll be more next week - see you then!

item.title: Weeknotes 202612
item.description:
There’ll be more next week - see you then!

... And in the end, there was not more next week or anything else for over a year. To cut the story short, because this is not a 2025 review, life happened. 2025 was not a bad year, by any means, but it was a hard working year. Normal parenting stuff - nothing out of the ordinary. Years usually start optimistically and I was convinced A's sleeping patterns would improve like H's around the second year and we, the parents, would be less sleep deprived, but this time it was different.

I have been planning (in my mind) a rewrite of the static site generator for most of 2025 and I finally decided to roll up my sleeves during the Christmas break. The main new features are

  1. Incremental builds
  2. Using git to save checkpoints and derive created and modified dates, instead of relying on the OS filesystem's ctime and mtime
  3. Generate RSS feeds

I had no idea what I was getting into with the incremental builds and how infinitely more complex the codebase has become. Fast forward to last week, it felt most functionality was there and the most obvious bugs were fixed. It is still in beta but I'm doing a soft launch, not announcing for now in any of the usual channels. I think it's just a matter of updating and polishing some of the slash pages and it should ready for the general audience.

I am hoping this will be a solid foundation of a slowly evolving platform. Developing the static site generator has been fun but now it's time to turn my attention to actually using it - writing content.

item.title: Weeknotes 202511
item.description:
• It was today the first Formula 1 Grand Prix of the season in Melbourne. I’m watching it as I write this week’s notes. It is being quite entertaining. The McLaren seems to be the class of the field, confirming the suspicions of the pre-season testing. It seems this year’s car is very good at not putting too much strain in the tires, slowing the rate of decay in performance and allowing the drivers to push for longer.
  • I was confident at the end of last year that very soon I’d have more time and energy because nights with the 1 year old were going to improve but it’s already mid-March and it seems the year is flying by and I haven’t crossed anything from the 2025 objectives yet.
  • I have been listening to the remix of Severance soundtrack, Music To Refine To, while working and it’s pretty good. I’ve never watched the show but everything I’ve seen about it looks great, right up my alley. On the other hand, I get the sense it would be a depressing watch as the premise hits too close to home.
  • There’ll be more next week - see you then!

item.title: Weeknotes 202508
item.description:
• Cold symptoms carried over from last week and turned into something quite annoying.
  • Ended up not doing my weekly pilgrimage to the Paddington office, which I enjoy, to avoid spreading viral load and hopefully to recover quicker.
  • Added some polish to the wiki. The front page is improved and the layout in mobile should feel a bit more comfortable.
  • It’s half-term week here in the UK and that usually means juggling a bit more family stuff but this time we’ve booked holiday clubs with decent open and closing times so it has worked well.
  • We are going to spend a week in Lisbon around Easter but we need to nail those dates down so that we can start organising the trip.
  • Obsidian is now free for commercial use, so I can move away from OneNote at work, which I’ve been using since I’ve been given a slap on the wrist for using Obsidian without a licence a while ago.
  • I find it hard to believe things got to this point but Apple disabled E2E encryption for some of their services for UK users.
  • Looking forward for the next week, cold symptoms-free!

item.title: Weeknotes 202510
item.description:
• It’s March, the meteorological spring has started, the weather has been lovely in this part of the planet. After the snow bells, crocuses and daffodils are everywhere and cherry trees are blossoming.
  • There was Formula 1 pre-season testing in Bahrain and this week we have the first race of the season in Melbourne. I have a lot to say about this but will spare my readership and just write a few commonplace ideas: the last season before major changes in regulation is usually the best with performance convergence across the field, where usually everyone asks themselves Why are we changing the formula if this is so good?. On the other hand, McLaren seems to have made another step forward in performance — still to be confirmed — and this season could be a duel between Norris and Piastri, reminiscent of Hamilton vs Rosberg in 2014.
  • I’ve ordered a Keychron K1 Pro with PBT keycaps and brown switches. Since I spend so much time typing, thought it would be nice to use something substantially better than the £20 mechanical keyboard I’ve been using in the past few years. It’s all about the small pleasures.
  • We used to slog almost 7 months straight after the Christmas break and spend the first holiday period around the end of June but for the first time in 18 years we’ll be spending Easter in Lisbon, to better align with the school holidays. I think it’s great.
  • Wish you all a fantastic week!
